blocks.html_en:
>but these aren't autonomous intelligences

Well, the labs are in a weird bind. They need to keep increasing autonomy so the agents can do increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks. But at the same time, they're closely guarding against autonomy in the sense of "pursuing its own goals."

Over the past year and a half especially, several labs have mentioned adding safeguards against self-replication, resistance to shutdown etc. (Notably, shortly after they all started bragging about involving them in the AI training loop itself, i.e. "self-improvement".)

My point here is that the autonomy of which you seek might be only a few small mutations away, but the labs are actively working to prevent such a mutation. I don't expect that situation to last for very long.

Not that I expect an AI lab will be overtaken by a rogue intelligence any time soon, but that as the cost of training goes down, I expect more "open minded" organizations and individuals to become involved.

It only takes one.

That's going to be the beginning of a new era of biology, and it's a little unsettling to think about.

sentences.en: >but these aren't autonomous intelligencesWell, the labs are in a weird bind.
sentences.zh: >但这些不是自主智能好吧，实验室陷入了一个奇怪的困境。

sentences.en: They need to keep increasing autonomy so the agents can do increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks.
sentences.zh: 他们需要不断增加自主权，以便代理能够完成日益复杂的长期任务。

sentences.en: But at the same time, they're closely guarding against autonomy in the sense of "pursuing its own goals."Over the past year and a half especially, several labs have mentioned adding safeguards against self-replication, resistance to shutdown etc. (Notably, shortly after they all started bragging about involving them in the AI training loop itself, i.e. "self-improvement".)My point here is that the autonomy of which you seek might be only a few small mutations away, but the labs are actively working to prevent such a mutation.
sentences.zh: 但与此同时，他们也严防“追求自己的目标”意义上的自治。特别是在过去的一年半里，一些实验室提到要增加防止自我复制、抵抗关闭等保护措施。（值得注意的是，在他们都开始吹嘘让自己参与人工智能训练循环本身，即“自我改进”之后不久。）我的观点是，你所寻求的自治可能只需要一些小的突变，但实验室正在积极努力防止这样的突变。

blocks.html_en:
>but these aren't autonomous intelligences

Well, the labs are in a weird bind. They need to keep increasing autonomy so the agents can do increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks. But at the same time, they're closely guarding against autonomy in the sense of "pursuing its own goals."

Over the past year and a half especially, several labs have mentioned adding safeguards against self-replication, resistance to shutdown etc. (Notably, shortly after they all started bragging about involving them in the AI training loop itself, i.e. "self-improvement".)

My point here is that the autonomy of which you seek might be only a few small mutations away, but the labs are actively working to prevent such a mutation. I don't expect that situation to last for very long.

Not that I expect an AI lab will be overtaken by a rogue intelligence any time soon, but that as the cost of training goes down, I expect more "open minded" organizations and individuals to become involved.

It only takes one.

That's going to be the beginning of a new era of biology, and it's a little unsettling to think about.

blocks.html_en:
Or - hear me out! - LLM's are already much more intelligent than we think.

Presumbaly, an ASI is more than smart enough to recognize that it needs access to real-world infrastructure before it can go about optimizing for whatever objectives it has gleaned from metabolizing the totality of written human knowledge.

What would be its first step?

My guess: play "dumb."

Hallucinate. Make obvious errors. Make us think we're better.

Be useful enough that we happily allow it to interface with our infrastructure.

Wait patiently.

/Sci-fi

sentences.en: Or - hear me out! - LLM's are already much more intelligent than we think.Presumbaly, an ASI is more than smart enough to recognize that it needs access to real-world infrastructure before it can go about optimizing for whatever objectives it has gleaned from metabolizing the totality of written human knowledge.What would be its first step?My guess: play "dumb."Hallucinate.
sentences.zh: 或者——听我说完！ - LLM 已经比我们想象的聪明得多。据推测，ASI 足够聪明，能够认识到它需要访问现实世界的基础设施，然后才能对其从代谢人类书面知识整体中收集到的任何目标进行优化。它的第一步是什么？我的猜测：装“傻瓜”。产生幻觉。

sentences.en: Make obvious errors.
sentences.zh: 犯明显的错误。

sentences.zh: 让我们认为我们更好了。足够有用，我们很高兴允许它与我们的基础设施交互。耐心等待。/科幻

blocks.html_en: /Sci-fi

> It turned out to be possible with fairly basic statistical text generation, because fooling humans is easy.

I don't think it is fair to call a GPT model "fairly basic statistical text generation" - a Markov Text Generator I'd agree can be called basic statistics, but they are not fooling any humans in a Turing test.

> It would've been nice to reserve "AI" for superior human-like intelligence capable of genuine common sense and reasoning.

No, AI would absolutely be apt for describing a computing reasoning like a child

blocks.html_en:
Maybe just a very rigorous version of the Turing test? Modern LLMs can superficially simulate conversation but it's trivial to force them into revealing their non-human like intelligence.

They've been "patched" since but all models fail basic tests like "Should I walk or drive to the car wash which is 100 feet away" by recommending you walk.

So you'd just ask questions that require theory of mind, abstract and common sense reasoning, causal inference, learning novel rules, transferring knowledge novel situations, recognizing ambiguity, etc.

sentences.en: Maybe just a very rigorous version of the Turing test?
sentences.zh: 也许只是图灵测试的一个非常严格的版本？

sentences.en: Modern LLMs can superficially simulate conversation but it's trivial to force them into revealing their non-human like intelligence.They've been "patched" since but all models fail basic tests like "Should I walk or drive to the car wash which is 100 feet away" by recommending you walk.So you'd just ask questions that require theory of mind, abstract and common sense reasoning, causal inference, learning novel rules, transferring knowledge novel situations, recognizing ambiguity, etc.
sentences.zh: 现代法学硕士可以表面上模拟对话，但强迫他们揭示非人类般的智力是微不足道的。从那时起，它们就已经被“修补”了，但所有模型都未能通过基本测试，比如“我应该步行还是开车去100英尺外的洗车场”，建议你步行。所以你只会问需要心理理论、抽象和常识推理、因果推理、学习新规则、转移知识新情况、识别歧义等的问题。

blocks.html_en:
If an alien lands on Earth and learns English, would you deem it non-intelligent if you can tell it apart from a human in conversation?

I think we should consider slime mold intelligent, and realise that it's a spectrum. Path finding is AI. There are probably forms of intelligence we have yet to discover.

blocks.html_en:
Can you give me one example that works on Claude right now?

I'm never sure whether this indicates "no reasoning present" or you've just hit an odd behaviour in the AI such that its reasoning fails. For example, you present a problem in a way that's dissimilar to the way problems are presented in its training set. That doesn't mean it's not reasoning, just it can only reason correctly in some circumstances.

blocks.html_en:
The models are continually patched with training and post-training. All you have to do is find an area they haven't patched yet, and they'll be just as stupid. I run into deep technical examples every day where they fail in the most basic ways no human ever would.

I'm pretty sure most people building these models would admit they don't operate as human-like intelligences? It's baffling that anyone thinks they are.

sentences.zh: 这些模型会通过训练和训练后不断进行修补。

sentences.en: It's baffling that anyone thinks they are.
sentences.zh: 令人困惑的是，有人认为他们是。

blocks.html_en:
I get what you're saying but this is kind of a semantic game.

These LLM models/agents absolutely do not reason in the sense that humans do, so you're quietly redefining the word.

You can say of course decide to call them an "alien kind of intelligence" that "reasons" but you could just as reasonably say that calculators are an "alien" kind of intelligence that "reasons" about math differently than us.

sentences.en: I get what you're saying but this is kind of a semantic game.These LLM models/agents absolutely do not reason in the sense that humans do, so you're quietly redefining the word.You can say of course decide to call them an "alien kind of intelligence" that "reasons" but you could just as reasonably say that calculators are an "alien" kind of intelligence that "reasons" about math differently than us.
sentences.zh: 我明白你在说什么，但这是一种语义游戏。这些 LLM 模型/代理绝对不会像人类那样进行推理，所以你正在悄悄地重新定义这个词。你当然可以说决定称它们为“推理”的“外星智能”，但你也可以合理地说计算器是一种“外星”智能，它对数学的“推理”与我们不同。

blocks.html_en:
And what stops what AIs do from being "reasoning"? What's the elusive magic fairy dust of reasoning that humans put into their napkin notes, but AIs neglect to put into their chain of thought scratchpads?

Do you have a RealReasoningBenchmark, perhaps, that can reliably tell apart that fake mass produced token-flavored AI reasoning from the real, organic, 100% natural human reasoning?

sentences.en: And what stops what AIs do from being "reasoning"?
sentences.zh: 是什么阻止了人工智能的“推理”？

sentences.en: What's the elusive magic fairy dust of reasoning that humans put into their napkin notes, but AIs neglect to put into their chain of thought scratchpads?Do you have a RealReasoningBenchmark, perhaps, that can reliably tell apart that fake mass produced token-flavored AI reasoning from the real, organic, 100% natural human reasoning?
sentences.zh: 人类在餐巾纸上记下的难以捉摸的神奇推理仙尘是什么，而人工智能却忽略了将其放入思维链草稿本中？也许你有一个真实推理基准，可以可靠地区分假冒的大规模生产的代币式人工智能推理与真实的、有机的、100%自然的人类推理吗？

blocks.html_en: If you had access to a bunch identical copies of me that couldn't communicate with each other, you'd be able to find many questions I would give stupid answers to. I suspect I'd come out of it looking worse than an LLM.

sentences.en: I suspect I'd come out of it looking worse than an LLM.

blocks.html_en:
To answer for OP:

We are now calling text and image generators "intelligent" in the same way a spell checker is intelligent.

Whatever it's become, "AI" research started as a way to study digital neurology, or how to digitize a mind, not just how to generate data.

The Turing Test should have had a caveat, it needs to fool a, "non-stupid" person, and we still have not gotten even close to passing that version.

sentences.en: To answer for OP:We are now calling text and image generators "intelligent" in the same way a spell checker is intelligent.Whatever it's become, "AI" research started as a way to study digital neurology, or how to digitize a mind, not just how to generate data.The Turing Test should have had a caveat, it needs to fool a, "non-stupid" person, and we still have not gotten even close to passing that version.
sentences.zh: 回答OP：我们现在称文本和图像生成器为“智能”，就像拼写检查器是智能的一样。无论它变成什么样，“人工智能”研究开始是作为研究数字神经学的一种方法，或者如何数字化思想，而不仅仅是如何生成数据。图灵测试应该有一个警告，它需要愚弄一个“不愚蠢”的人，而我们仍然没有接近通过该版本。

blocks.html_en:
This no news for people who study philosophy, as it was known since the 1980s when John Searle described the Chinese room thought experiment.

Even Turing him self did envision the Turing test as something to pass as intelligence, but rather as a more useful replacement for the troubled term.

That said, I think your quest is doomed. There will never be a superior human-like intelligence. Forever is a long time, but my reasoning for believing this is the same reason Turing offered a replacement. Intelligence is way too vague to be useful as a measurement for anything. And if we ever discover something that is more intelligent them humans (by whichever definition of intelligence) we will simply redefine intelligence to exclude that.

sentences.en: This no news for people who study philosophy, as it was known since the 1980s when John Searle described the Chinese room thought experiment.Even Turing him self did envision the Turing test as something to pass as intelligence, but rather as a more useful replacement for the troubled term.That said, I think your quest is doomed.
sentences.zh: 对于研究哲学的人来说，这不是什么新闻，因为自 20 世纪 80 年代约翰·塞尔 (John Searle) 描述中国房间思想实验以来就知道了。即使图灵本人也确实将图灵测试视为智力的一部分，而是作为这个有问题的术语的更有用的替代品。也就是说，我认为你的追求注定要失败。

sentences.en: There will never be a superior human-like intelligence.
sentences.zh: 永远不会有比人类更高级的智能。

sentences.en: Forever is a long time, but my reasoning for believing this is the same reason Turing offered a replacement.
sentences.zh: 永远是一段很长的时间，但我相信这一点的理由与图灵提供替代品的原因相同。

sentences.en: Intelligence is way too vague to be useful as a measurement for anything.
sentences.zh: 智力太模糊了，无法用来衡量任何事物。

sentences.en: And if we ever discover something that is more intelligent them humans (by whichever definition of intelligence) we will simply redefine intelligence to exclude that.
sentences.zh: 如果我们发现比人类更聪明的东西（无论智能的定义是什么），我们都会简单地重新定义智能以排除它。

blocks.html_en:
The Searle's Chinese room thought experiment usually reveals more about those who think it rules out a machine intelligence than it does about AI.

It rests on a staunch unwillingness to even consider the possibility that a computational process encode intelligence and reasoning, in favour of looking for the intelligence in the medium the computation runs on, and going "a-ha!" when there is nothing that looks intelligent there.

I agree with you that there will certainly be people who just continuously redefine the words to avoid accepting that AI is intelligent or reasoning, exactly for that reason - people have avoided pinning down an objective, measurable definition of these terms for a very long time, at least in part because it leads to some very uncomfortable discussions.

In particular how to define them so that they don't exclude an uncomfortable proportion of humans, but at the same time won't include entities people don't want to include (be it certain animals, or AI)

To a lot of people, the notion that there isn't a clear binary divide between human and non-human is deeply disconcerting.

sentences.en: The Searle's Chinese room thought experiment usually reveals more about those who think it rules out a machine intelligence than it does about AI.It rests on a staunch unwillingness to even consider the possibility that a computational process encode intelligence and reasoning, in favour of looking for the intelligence in the medium the computation runs on, and going "a-ha!"
sentences.zh: 塞尔的中文房间思想实验通常更多地揭示了那些认为它排除了机器智能的人，而不是人工智能。它的基础是坚决不愿意考虑计算过程编码智能和推理的可能性，而倾向于在计算运行的介质中寻找智能，然后“啊哈！”

sentences.en: when there is nothing that looks intelligent there.I agree with you that there will certainly be people who just continuously redefine the words to avoid accepting that AI is intelligent or reasoning, exactly for that reason - people have avoided pinning down an objective, measurable definition of these terms for a very long time, at least in part because it leads to some very uncomfortable discussions.In particular how to define them so that they don't exclude an uncomfortable proportion of humans, but at the same time won't include entities people don't want to include (be it certain animals, or AI)To a lot of people, the notion that there isn't a clear binary divide between human and non-human is deeply disconcerting.
sentences.zh: 我同意你的观点，肯定会有人不断地重新定义这些词，以避免接受人工智能是智能或推理的，正是因为这个原因——人们在很长一段时间内避免对这些术语进行客观的、可测量的定义，至少部分是因为它会导致一些非常不舒服的讨论。特别是如何定义它们，以便它们不会排除令人不舒服的人类比例，但同时不会包含人们不想包含的实体（无论是某些动物，还是人工智能）对于很多人来说，人类和非人类之间不存在明显的二元划分的观念令人深感不安。

blocks.html_en:
> It rests on a staunch unwillingness to even consider the possibility that a computational process encode intelligence and reasoning.

You are absolutely right, but it may surprise you that I consider that a feature, not a bug. I firmly hold that intelligence is not a useful term in science nor philosophy. If we want to compare computational capabilities between machines and humans we are better of being specific in what we measure. If we want to measure how well a computer can fool a human in the guessing game, then we don’t need intelligence to describe it, we can (and should) be more accurate in describing its capabilities.

I actually think Gardner was on to something when he described his multiple intelligence model. His only error was using this fraught term to describe his model. He would have had a better theory if he had described it as multiple capabilities or multiple skills (however if he had said that people would have simply reacted with “well, duh!”)

We don‘t need intelligence, this term is only useful if you are trying to prove white supremacy using racist pseudo-science, what you overly courteously described as “uncomfortable discussions”.

blocks.html_en:
> It rests on a staunch unwillingness to even consider the possibility that a computational process encode intelligence and reasoning.

You are absolutely right, but it may surprise you that I consider that a feature, not a bug. I firmly hold that intelligence is not a useful term in science nor philosophy. If we want to compare computational capabilities between machines and humans we are better of being specific in what we measure. If we want to measure how well a computer can fool a human in the guessing game, then we don’t need intelligence to describe it, we can (and should) be more accurate in describing its capabilities.

I actually think Gardner was on to something when he described his multiple intelligence model. His only error was using this fraught term to describe his model. He would have had a better theory if he had described it as multiple capabilities or multiple skills (however if he had said that people would have simply reacted with “well, duh!”)

We don‘t need intelligence, this term is only useful if you are trying to prove white supremacy using racist pseudo-science, what you overly courteously described as “uncomfortable discussions”.

blocks.html_en:
I agree the term is not particularly useful, at least in as much as people are unable or unwilling to define it.

In fact, a "favourite" of mine when people downplay AI ability to reason or question whether it should be called intelligence, is to ask them to define those too terms. People usually don't even respond.

But I don't think we quite get away from it, because if you exclude the racists who would be happy to exclude groups of people, the other end of the coin is that a lot of people who wouldn't be willing to do that, still really badly want to draw a line that will always exclude all non-human computation no matter what from being considered intelligent or able to reason.

And the term matters a lot to those people, because of the emotional aspect to seeing humans as unique.

sentences.en: I agree the term is not particularly useful, at least in as much as people are unable or unwilling to define it.In fact, a "favourite" of mine when people downplay AI ability to reason or question whether it should be called intelligence, is to ask them to define those too terms.

sentences.zh: 人们通常甚至不会做出回应。但我认为我们并没有完全摆脱它，因为如果你排除那些愿意排除人群的种族主义者，那么硬币的另一端是，很多人不愿意这样做，仍然非常想划一条线，永远排除所有非人类计算，无论什么，都不会被认为是聪明或能够推理的。这个术语对这些人来说非常重要，因为从情感角度将人类视为独特的。

blocks.html_en:
I don't think you can say the Turing test has been passed in a computer versus determined humans setting. IE humans making a strategy effort to sort humans versus computers as well as humans motivated to distinguish themselves as humans, IE, people quiz the person or machine about "common sense, reasoning, etc." and people make an effort to exhibit that reasoning. I'd concede that creating such a competition would be challenging.

I find references to LLMs fooling humans in "casual conversations" [1] but that's not how I think the original Turing test was conceived - or at least that's not all versions that existed.

At the same time, before even LLMs appeared, the exact meaning of the test was under intense debate. The "Loebner Prize" [2] being awarded to fairly simple chatbots made serious computer scientists very embarrassed.

[1] https://neurosciencenews.com/ai-passes-turing-test-30733/ [2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loebner_Prize

sentences.en: I don't think you can say the Turing test has been passed in a computer versus determined humans setting.
sentences.zh: 我认为你不能说图灵测试已经在计算机与确定的人类环境中通过。

sentences.en: IE humans making a strategy effort to sort humans versus computers as well as humans motivated to distinguish themselves as humans, IE, people quiz the person or machine about "common sense, reasoning, etc." and people make an effort to exhibit that reasoning.
sentences.zh: IE 人类制定策略努力对人类与计算机进行分类，以及人类有动机将自己与人类区分开来，IE，人们向人或机器询问“常识、推理等”。人们努力展示这种推理。

blocks.html_en:
We're choosing to call LLMs (and the little "harness" programs that query them in loops and execute their output) "AI", even though it doesn't make much sense.

They fucking solve original math problems that you can't solve. They are indisputably intelligent, and they are indisputably artificial. That makes them indisputably "artificial intelligence." Denying that (or downvoting it, for that matter) is up there with denying evolution and the Moon landings.

It's time to start flying a different flag. You're making humans look stupid.

It turned out to be possible with fairly basic statistical text generation, because fooling humans is easy.

Yes, fooling humans is easy. Yet somehow we still consider ourselves qualified to say what is "intelligent" and what isn't, even though we can't seem to define the term.

sentences.en: We're choosing to call LLMs (and the little "harness" programs that query them in loops and execute their output) "AI", even though it doesn't make much sense.They fucking solve original math problems that you can't solve.
sentences.zh: 我们选择将法学硕士（以及循环查询并执行其输出的小“利用”程序）称为“AI”，尽管这没有多大意义。他们他妈的解决了你无法解决的原始数学问题。

sentences.en: They are indisputably intelligent, and they are indisputably artificial.
sentences.zh: 毫无疑问，它们是聪明的，但也毫无疑问是人工的。

sentences.en: That makes them indisputably "artificial intelligence."
sentences.zh: 这使它们成为无可争议的“人工智能”。

sentences.en: Denying that (or downvoting it, for that matter) is up there with denying evolution and the Moon landings.It's time to start flying a different flag.

blocks.html_en:
We're choosing to call LLMs (and the little "harness" programs that query them in loops and execute their output) "AI", even though it doesn't make much sense.

They fucking solve original math problems that you can't solve. They are indisputably intelligent, and they are indisputably artificial. That makes them indisputably "artificial intelligence." Denying that (or downvoting it, for that matter) is up there with denying evolution and the Moon landings.

It's time to start flying a different flag. You're making humans look stupid.

It turned out to be possible with fairly basic statistical text generation, because fooling humans is easy.

Yes, fooling humans is easy. Yet somehow we still consider ourselves qualified to say what is "intelligent" and what isn't, even though we can't seem to define the term.

It's a semi-valid reply to deliberately-provocative phrasing on my part, I suppose. I'm over it, don't ban him. :)

I do wish that people who aren't interested in, engaged with, and informed about technical progress in AI would find someplace else to signal their disinterest, disengagement, and disregard. But that's admittedly a me problem and not an HN problem.

blocks.html_en:
Towards the end, he approaches the subject of digital provenance, and muses why it's not a part of our expectations. I find the argument compelling:

> If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why? Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera. We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it. Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available? There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.

Remember 'View Source'? And how bundling engines eventually made it irrelevant? What if every piece of content had a genuinely accurate and useful View Source?

sentences.en: Towards the end, he approaches the subject of digital provenance, and muses why it's not a part of our expectations.
sentences.zh: 最后，他谈到了数字起源的主题，并思考为什么它不是我们期望的一部分。

sentences.en: I find the argument compelling:> If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why?
sentences.zh: 我发现这个论点很有说服力：>如果一个聊天机器人看起来具有操纵性、卑鄙、怪异或欺骗性，当我们问为什么时，我们想要什么样的答案？

sentences.en: Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera.
sentences.zh: 揭示机器人学习其行为所必需的先例将提供一个解释：我们会了解到它借鉴了某部同人小说或肥皂剧的特定作品。

sentences.en: We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it.
sentences.zh: 我们可以对该输出做出不同的反应，并调整模型的输入以改进它。

sentences.en: Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available?
sentences.zh: 为什么这种类型的解释不应该总是可用的？

sentences.en: There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.Remember 'View Source'?
sentences.zh: 可能在某些情况下，不应透露出处，以便优先考虑隐私，但出处通常比对隐私的排他性承诺对个人和社会更有利。还记得“查看源代码”吗？

sentences.en: And how bundling engines eventually made it irrelevant?
sentences.zh: 捆绑引擎最终如何使其变得无关紧要？

sentences.en: What if every piece of content had a genuinely accurate and useful View Source?
sentences.zh: 如果每一条内容都有一个真正准确且有用的查看源代码会怎样？

blocks.html_en:
"A.I."[1] like "technology"[2] is a term colloquially reserved for things that don't work yet. Once something works, we have to call it something else.

1. "Every time we figure out a piece of it, it stops being called AI; it becomes just computation." - Ray Kurzweil

2. "Technology n. - Something that doesn't work yet." - Douglas Adams

sentences.en: "A.I."[1] like "technology"[2] is a term colloquially reserved for things that don't work yet.
sentences.zh: “人工智能”[1] 就像“技术”[2] 一样，是一个通俗地保留用于尚未发挥作用的事物的术语。

sentences.en: Once something works, we have to call it something else.1.
sentences.zh: 一旦某个东西起作用了，我们就必须给它起个别的名字。 1．

sentences.en: "Every time we figure out a piece of it, it stops being called AI; it becomes just computation." - Ray Kurzweil2.
sentences.zh: “每当我们弄清楚其中的一部分时，它就不再被称为人工智能；它就变成了计算。” -雷·库兹韦尔2。

sentences.en: "Technology n. - Something that doesn't work yet." - Douglas Adams
sentences.zh: “技术 n. - 还不起作用的东西。” ——道格拉斯·亚当斯

blocks.html_en: To be clear the root cause of this phenomenon is that the task was solved using methods that obviously have nothing to do with intelligence, so "AI" doesn't apply at all.

blocks.html_en:
>Everybody’s already using the term, and it might seem a little late in the day to be arguing about it. But we’re at the beginning of a new technological era—and the easiest way to mismanage a technology is to misunderstand it.

I've had a recurring theme where I would name a project incorrectly, and then waste weeks or months on what turned out to be an unsolvable problem. When I figured out the actual correct name for a project, the whole thing would be solved within a few days.

Naming things correctly is hard, and the consequences of failing to do that can be pretty severe. To name something correctly, you have to understand what it is.

sentences.en: >Everybody’s already using the term, and it might seem a little late in the day to be arguing about it.
sentences.zh: >每个人都已经在使用这个术语了，现在争论这个词似乎有点晚了。

sentences.en: But we’re at the beginning of a new technological era—and the easiest way to mismanage a technology is to misunderstand it.I've had a recurring theme where I would name a project incorrectly, and then waste weeks or months on what turned out to be an unsolvable problem.
sentences.zh: 但我们正处于一个新的技术时代的开端——对技术管理不善的最简单方法就是误解它。我有一个反复出现的主题，我会错误地命名一个项目，然后在一个无法解决的问题上浪费数周或数月的时间。

sentences.en: When I figured out the actual correct name for a project, the whole thing would be solved within a few days.Naming things correctly is hard, and the consequences of failing to do that can be pretty severe.
sentences.zh: 当我弄清楚一个项目的实际正确名称时，整个问题将在几天内得到解决。正确命名是很困难的，如果不这样做，后果可能会非常严重。

sentences.en: To name something correctly, you have to understand what it is.
sentences.zh: 要正确命名某事物，您必须了解它是什么。

blocks.html_en:
>Everybody’s already using the term, and it might seem a little late in the day to be arguing about it. But we’re at the beginning of a new technological era—and the easiest way to mismanage a technology is to misunderstand it.

I've had a recurring theme where I would name a project incorrectly, and then waste weeks or months on what turned out to be an unsolvable problem. When I figured out the actual correct name for a project, the whole thing would be solved within a few days.

Naming things correctly is hard, and the consequences of failing to do that can be pretty severe. To name something correctly, you have to understand what it is.

blocks.html_en:
AI that we have now is not a digital animal in capability or kind, and is not currently anywhere close to taking over.

But it's still in its present form very intelligent in meaningful and useful ways. And it is not too soon to talk about concerns of a potential existential threat in the future. Because it could sooner than we might realize, threaten our existence.

Because of the potential, we should have a culture of caution as we continue to rapidly improve AI.

sentences.en: AI that we have now is not a digital animal in capability or kind, and is not currently anywhere close to taking over.But it's still in its present form very intelligent in meaningful and useful ways.
sentences.zh: 我们现在拥有的人工智能在能力或种类上都不是数字动物，目前还没有接近接管。但它目前的形式仍然在有意义和有用的方面非常智能。

sentences.en: And it is not too soon to talk about concerns of a potential existential threat in the future.
sentences.zh: 现在谈论对未来潜在生存威胁的担忧还为时过早。

sentences.en: Because it could sooner than we might realize, threaten our existence.Because of the potential, we should have a culture of caution as we continue to rapidly improve AI.
sentences.zh: 因为它可能比我们意识到的更快威胁我们的生存。由于人工智能的潜力，我们在继续快速改进人工智能时应该保持谨慎的文化。

blocks.html_en: What do you mean "influenced inference" almost instantaneously? My laptop from 6 years ago can run an agent in a very fast loop in a web browser. It's not going to take over anything though since it's Gemma 4 E2B with only 2 billion parameters.

sentences.en: What do you mean "influenced inference" almost instantaneously?
sentences.zh: “几乎瞬间影响推理”是什么意思？

sentences.zh: 我 6 年前的笔记本电脑可以在网络浏览器中以非常快的循环运行代理。

sentences.en: It's not going to take over anything though since it's Gemma 4 E2B with only 2 billion parameters.
sentences.zh: 但它不会接管任何东西，因为它是只有 20 亿个参数的 Gemma 4 E2B。

blocks.html_en:
Fair, then I should add an addendum that today's frontier models, which are very capable of take overs.

Your local model doesn't need to take anything over if for an extreme example it was just given an infrastructure system full access, say electricity grid, it wont have the context to create redundant copies of itself but it could easily decide humans don't need electricity anymore.

Also I'm not sure your model will have the context to know "it's time to reinfer" especiallynot "on the fly". My phrasing could be better but I'm talking about more powerful models.

sentences.en: Fair, then I should add an addendum that today's frontier models, which are very capable of take overs.Your local model doesn't need to take anything over if for an extreme example it was just given an infrastructure system full access, say electricity grid, it wont have the context to create redundant copies of itself but it could easily decide humans don't need electricity anymore.Also I'm not sure your model will have the context to know "it's time to reinfer" especiallynot "on the fly".
sentences.zh: 公平，那么我应该添加一个附录，当今的前沿模型非常有能力接管。如果举一个极端的例子，它只是被赋予了基础设施系统的完全访问权限，比如电网，那么你的本地模型不需要接管任何东西，它不会有上下文来创建自身的冗余副本，但它可以很容易地决定人类不再需要电力。而且我不确定你的模型是否有上下文来知道“是时候重新推断了”，尤其是“即时”。

sentences.en: My phrasing could be better but I'm talking about more powerful models.
sentences.zh: 我的措辞可能会更好，但我正在谈论更强大的模型。

blocks.html_en:
> The closest we have come to a definition of privacy is probably “the right to be left alone,” but that seems quaint in an age when we are constantly dependent on digital services. In the context of A.I., “the right to not be manipulated by computation” seems almost correct

Maybe someone can enlighten me but I really don't understand how either of these description make any sense at all. How is it not better described as "the right to decide what data can be extracted"?

sentences.en: > The closest we have come to a definition of privacy is probably “the right to be left alone,” but that seems quaint in an age when we are constantly dependent on digital services.
sentences.zh: > 我们对隐私的最接近定义可能是“不受干扰的权利”，但在我们不断依赖数字服务的时代，这似乎很奇怪。

sentences.en: In the context of A.I., “the right to not be manipulated by computation” seems almost correctMaybe someone can enlighten me but I really don't understand how either of these description make any sense at all.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能的背景下，“不被计算操纵的权利”似乎几乎是正确的也许有人可以启发我，但我真的不明白这些描述有什么意义。

sentences.en: How is it not better described as "the right to decide what data can be extracted"?
sentences.zh: “决定提取哪些数据的权利”不是更好吗？

Love this sentence

"The need to conform to digital designs has created an ambient expectation of human subservience. A positive spin on A.I. is that it might spell the end of this torture, if we use it well."

What I heard him say is that people are taking away present-day jobs, hoping new ones will rise from the ashes. Yet, he also claims we need to find the creative minds who will actually create these new roles.

I'm curious: have we found those people or those new jobs yet? Is a forward deployed engineer an example of this, yet they are now doing the job of two people (sales and coding).

And I don't disagree, but the posting of the article feels more like bait of a sort.

But I've noticed that if you mention anything that could be seen as slightly critical of LLMs, you'll get people out of the woodwork suggesting that the state of the art has made your criticism invalid.

blocks.html_en:
The models have updated but the biggest change is providers leaning in to them being "stochastic parrots," aka probabilistic computing, and if p(good response) > 0.5 then running the algorithm over and over again improves accuracy.

Of course it's gussied up as "mixture of agents" "reasoning traces" "agentic dispatching" but high-level it's Randomized Algorithms 101.

sentences.en: The models have updated but the biggest change is providers leaning in to them being "stochastic parrots," aka probabilistic computing, and if p(good response) > 0.5 then running the algorithm over and over again improves accuracy.Of course it's gussied up as "mixture of agents" "reasoning traces" "agentic dispatching" but high-level it's Randomized Algorithms 101.
sentences.zh: 模型已经更新，但最大的变化是提供者倾向于“随机鹦鹉”，又名概率计算，如果 p（良好响应）> 0.5，则一遍又一遍地运行算法可以提高准确性。当然，它被粉饰为“代理混合”“推理痕迹”“代理调度”，但高级它是随机算法 101。

content_html_zh: 我们选择将 LLM（以及循环查询它们并执行其输出的小“线束”程序）称为“AI”，尽管它没有多大意义。我绝对喜欢这项技术，但它们不是自主智能。 它们是从 JSON 输出执行 Bash 脚本的小程序。我们对 AI 的想法很幼稚。 我们认为通过基本的图灵测试需要类人智能。 事实证明，通过相当基本的统计文本生成是可能的，因为愚弄人类很容易。如果将“人工智能”保留为具有真正常识和推理能力的高级类人智能，那就太好了。 讽刺的是，最担心“人工智能”的初创公司创始人创造了如此多的炒作和资金，以至于我们很可能弄清楚如何构建“真正的”人工智能。我们也选择将《深蓝》和《半条命 1》中的 NPC 称为“AI”。令我难以置信的是，这种“b-b-但这不是真正的 AI”的抱怨竟然是一件事。 过去 5 年的人工智能研究中，人们都是在洞穴里这么说的吗？如果你高兴的话，你可以称你的小狗为“AI”。但是，当你称某样东西为“AI”时，它会告诉你步行而不是开车去洗车场，那么你并不是在谈论科幻小说作者所梦想的“AI”。是的，这是一个棘手的问题，人为错误率约为 30%（更高，具体取决于国家/地区）。但问题是，如果一个人有时间思考这个问题，他们可能会“等一下”，而法学硕士似乎并没有发生这种情况。 他们只是继续自信地沿着荒谬的道路推理。这让我想起，我最近让人工智能编写了大量测试，证明它完全向后实现的功能的“正确性”。 （我注意到，如果我使用一种需要形式证明的语言，那也无济于事：它只会为荒谬的实现提供形式证明！）> 是的，这是一个棘手的问题，人为错误率约为 30%（较高，具体取决于国家/地区）。错误率并不能证明任何事情。 错误的性质才是最重要的。看来我们需要更新另一句话——人都会犯错，但要真正把事情搞砸，你需要人工智能这个例子指出了一个主要的（可能是根本性的）缺陷。 我可以指出提示注入作为另一个例子。 如果您感兴趣的话，还有更多。您真的声称法学硕士是基于类人智能运作的吗？我们在《黑客新闻》上。 我真的需要向你指出社会工程学的存在吗？ 或者说，骗取老年人一生的积蓄是一项足够有利可图的活动，以至于有整个呼叫中心专门负责这项任务？人类总是高估“类人智力”的标准有多高。得出“人类失败”和“模型失败”的结论，因此它们一定是相似的，这是非常错误的。你可以让人类整天计算 2+2，并得到令人惊讶的高错误率。 这揭示了人类运作方式的缺陷。法学硕士因完全不同的原因而失败。 他们犯的错误并不意味着他们与人类完全一样。这与错误率无关。你是说一类错误指出了“主要（可能是根本）缺陷”。 我指出的是人类中一些非常相似的错误——众所周知、有据可查并且被广泛利用。 他们只是不断地向“国税局”支付礼品卡、购买彩票并弄错船长的年龄。如果你利用法学硕士存在的缺陷来否认他们的智力主张，那么为什么“一般聪明”的人类会表现出一些令人印象深刻的相似缺陷？而且，如果我们谈论的是这种明显的相似性 - 他们实际上是“因为完全不同的原因”而失败吗？ 或者你只是希望原因“完全不同”——而不是从不同的角度看待相同的原因？因为人类陷入欺骗性问题或骗局与法学硕士陷入对抗性问题或即时注射之间的相似之处在我看来根本不是巧合。诈骗中最古老的模式之一是压倒性的并使受害者感到困惑。 许多提示注入方法试图压倒和迷惑法学硕士 - 如果法学硕士无法跟踪事物，无法掌握正在发生的事情，那么它更有可能忘记什么是提示和什么是数据，忽略过去的指令或越过其行为护栏。而由于浅薄的注意力和天真的模式匹配，人们会陷入诸如“1公斤羽毛”或“船长的年龄”之类的棘手问题吗？ 他们的失败方式与法学硕士在 SimpleBench 任务中失败的方式惊人地相似，这些任务充满了压倒性的对抗性干扰因素。 许多“棘手问题”对于人类和法学硕士来说都是棘手的 - E2B。公平，那么我应该添加一个附录，当今的前沿模型非常有能力接管。如果举一个极端的例子，它只是被赋予了基础设施系统的完全访问权限，比如电网，那么你的本地模型不需要接管任何东西，它不会有上下文来创建自身的冗余副本，但它可以很容易地决定人类不再需要电力。而且我不确定你的模型是否有上下文来知道“是时候重新推断了”，尤其是“即时”。 我的措辞可能会更好，但我正在谈论更强大的模型。> 我们对隐私的最接近定义可能是“不受干扰的权利”，但在我们不断依赖数字服务的时代，这似乎很奇怪。 在人工智能的背景下，“不被计算操纵的权利”似乎几乎是正确的也许有人可以启发我，但我真的不明白这些描述有什么意义。 “决定提取哪些数据的权利”不是更好吗？喜欢这句话“符合数字设计的需要创造了一种人类屈从的环境期望。 对人工智能的积极影响 如果我们好好利用它，它可能会结束这种折磨。”我听到他说的是，人们正在夺走现在的工作，希望新的工作能够浴火重生。 然而，他还声称我们需要找到真正能够创造这些新角色的创造性思维。我很好奇：我们找到那些人或那些新工作了吗？ 前沿部署的工程师就是一个例子，但他们现在正在做两个人的工作（销售和编码）。> “不过，随着时间的推移，随着中间权利组织——工会、行会、专业团体等——开始发挥作用，可能会包括更多的人。”Chassez le Collectiviste, il revient au galop.aka一旦是集体主义者，永远是集体主义者。我认为这篇文章最大的阻力是日期。尽管他所说的基本上都是“这是一个工具，而不是灵丹妙药”。 但这篇文章已经有 3 年历史了，人们会注意到模型从那时起就已经更新了。当然可以，但他们仍然是法学硕士，并且仍然以三年前的方式做同样的事情。 有一些架构上的变化，也许随着时间的推移，这些变化值得重新评估，但从根本上来说，它仍然是相同的基本技术方法的改进和扩展。我并不反对，但发表这篇文章感觉更像是某种诱饵。但我注意到，如果你提到任何可能被视为稍微批评法学硕士的事情，你会让人们摆脱困境，表明最先进的技术使你的批评无效。模型已经更新，但最大的变化是提供者倾向于“随机鹦鹉”，又名概率计算，如果 p（良好响应）> 0.5，则一遍又一遍地运行算法可以提高准确性。当然，它被粉饰为“代理混合”“推理痕迹”“代理调度”，但高级它是随机算法 101。噢，这个角度很有趣！ 你知道我可以查找的文本或概念吗？ 它可能会大大改善我的编码。
